interactions.label: Specular Spread α
interactions.teaches: Adjusting α shows how spread or focused the specular highlight becomes, demonstrating the impact of Phong's specular exponent on shininess.

interactions.label: Rotate Sphere
interactions.teaches: Rotating the sphere changes the angles at which light reflects, revealing different aspects of Lambertian vs. specular reflection.

demo_steps.title: Understanding Lambertian Reflection
demo_steps.narration: The sphere you see is rendered using Lambertian reflection. Notice how the light is diffusely scattered in all directions, giving it a matte appearance.

demo_steps.title: Specular Highlight Introduction
demo_steps.narration: Now, increase the specular exponent α slightly. Notice the appearance of a shiny highlight where the light direction closely aligns with the viewing direction.

demo_steps.title: Focusing the Specular Reflection
demo_steps.narration: As α increases further, the highlight becomes smaller and more intense, demonstrating how sharper reflections appear on shinier surfaces.

plan.camera_suggestion: Front view centered on the sphere, ensuring a clear view of the reflection vectors and highlight effects. Maintain a scale that mirrors the original's apparent size and detail.

plan.notes: Ensure that the lighting setup and resulting reflections are calibrated to closely mimic the original shading characteristics from the provided image.

plan.contextChunk:
all reflections; (2) a diffuse component, which is the Lambertian
reflection of @eq-Lambert; and (3) a specular reflection component. For a given ray
direction, $\mathbf{q}$, from the surface, the Phong specular
contribution, $\ell_{\mbox{Phong spec}}$, is:
$$\ell_{\mbox{Phong spec}} = k_s (\mathbf{r} \cdot \mathbf{q})^\alpha \ell_{\texttt{in}},$$
where $k_s$ is a constant, $\alpha$ is a parameter governing the spread
of the specular reflection, and the unit vector $\mathbf{r}$ denotes the
direction of maximum specular reflection, given by
$$\mathbf{r} = 2(\mathbf{p} \cdot \mathbf{n}) \mathbf{n} - \mathbf{p}$$

@fig-rendering shows the ambient, Lambertian, and Phong shading components of a sphere
under two-source illumination, and a comparison with a real sphere under
similar real illumination.

:::{layout-ncol="3" #fig-rendering}
![Lambertian](figures/imaging/sphereDiffuse.png){width="90%" #fig-rendering-a}

![Phong](figures/imaging/spherePhongRoughness0.3.png){width="90%" #fig-rendering-b}

![Photograph](figures/imaging/photoSphere.jpg){width="90%" #fig-rendering-c}

Fig (a and b) Two different renderings of a sphere.  (c) Note the many extra details required of a photorealistic rendering.

:::

In general, surface reflection behaves linearly: the reflection from the
sum of two light sources is the sum of the reflections from the
individual sources.

To associate the reflected light with surfaces in the world, we need to
know which light rays came from which direction in space. That requires

discrepancies: The source image has two distinct tones with noticeable shading, whereas the generated version is uniformly lit.
The source sphere appears more spherical and aligned straight on, whereas the generated sphere's shape looks flatter.
Background color controls overlay the scene, which doesn’t match the source figure design.
evaluation.notes: The generated figure lacks shading differentiation, sphere shape accuracy, and appropriate background control handling.
action_items: Adjust the lighting to more accurately replicate the shading of the source image.
Ensure sphere geometry matches the depiction in the source image, with better alignment and proportions.
Redesign or remove the background color control to better integrate it without overlaying the rendered figure.
